The Boltzmann equation is one of the most fundamental kinetic equations which
describes the time evolution of the particle probability density distribution. Under
proper scaling, the dimensionless Boltzmann equation reads as
1
(2.1) ε∂ f +v·∇ f −E·∇ f = Q(f),
t x v ε
wheref(x,v,t)istheprobabilitydensityfunctionofparticlesatpositionx∈Rd with
velocity v ∈ Rd at time t > 0, with d representing the dimension of the computa-
tional field. The constant ε is the Knudsen number, and E is the electric field. The
Boltzmann collision operator Q(f) is given by
(cid:90)
(2.2) Q(f)= σ(v,w)(M(v)f(w)−M(w)f(v))dw,
R
where σ is the anisotropic scattering kernel satisfying σ(v,w) = σ(w,v) > 0, and M
is the normalized Maxwellian distribution defined as
(2.3) M(v)= √ 1 e−v 2 2 .
2π
We further define the collision frequency λ as
(cid:90)
(2.4) λ(x,v)= σ(v,w)M(w)dw,
R
and assume that it satisfies λ ≤ µ for some positive constant µ. More details about
this model can be found in [25].
In the following subsections, we briefly review the process to formulate the dif-
fusive relaxation system based on the even-odd decomposition method [20–22] and
introduce the AP temporal discretization scheme, as a preparation for deriving the
AP fully discrete scheme in Section 3. For simplicity of discussion, we consider one-
dimensional velocity variable v ∈R as an illustrative example to show the main idea.
We refer readers to [20–22] for more details.

split (2.1) into two equations for v and −v, respectively,

Let ∆t denote the time step size and (rn,jn) denote the solution at n-th time
level. The temporal scheme for advancing (rn,jn) to (rn+1,jn+1) consists of schemes
for updating (rn,jn) to the intermediate solution (r∗,j∗) by the relaxation step and
for updating (r∗,j∗) to (rn+1,jn+1) by the transport step.
For the relaxation step (2.10) which involves the challenge arising from the stiff-
